speaker: interviewer
text: March 29th, 1996, in Queens, New York, USA, and the interviewer is [? Marianne ?] [? Weisburg. ?] So you are in Bari on your way, hopefully, to Sweden.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Sweden. So anyhow, we had been there for another three weeks with a lot of ins and outs, and big jealousy from the other people because we were so segregated there. And then we got-- we had to get out of there, and the only way we could do-- we finally got a wonderful American sergeant who was allowed to get three days off if he takes us to Rome. And we went through Monte Cassino, which was just after the terrible fight there was, and they still had dead animals and all that, but the [INAUDIBLE] trees were already coming up. And we went all the way to Rome. And in Rome, we did have a hotel room, and it was fascinating. We stayed a whole-- a whole month in Rome. Then we went to Switzerland, where we stayed another month. And that was not a very nice month, because the Swiss people didn't believe a word we told them. They said such a man didn't exist and so forth. It was very unpleasant. And finally, I got a phone call that the Norwegian Red Cross will take us through bombed-out Germany to Denmark, and from there we can go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we did that, but thanks God I was looking, and the Swiss took every piece of food and money away from everybody. And they knew that we had to go through Germany-- three days when there was no food and no nothing. So I went into the bathroom and put my money into my shoe. At least I had that money.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, yes, it took us-- and that was horrifying, because it really was bombed out, and we-- we watched the Germans walking like that and the Americans on the side, you know, taking care of them. And we got to Denmark, and what the Danes did, that was incredible. They made up a camp for us. There was Norwegians, Danes, Swedes, and some Austrians who were stuck somewhere, and they all went on these Norwegians. But the funny part was that the bus drivers were all German war prisoners, and nobody wanted to talk German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put me down. And I had to laugh all the way, because I had to tell him where not to go, because in English it says no bridge, no this. And I said, if you would have known that a Jew tell him what to do, it probably would have killed him, but he didn't. So as we got there, we got-- I mean, they must have put up 2,000 shower rooms for us and food. It was wonderful. And so we were in Denmark for just about two days, I think. Not longer. And then, from Denmark, all our-- six of the Swedes had been flown over to Sweden.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only three of us-- the woman who spoke German, a Hungarian boy who was married to a Swedish woman, and me-- we were staying there. So we went on the same boat trip, how the Danish Jews were taken out to Germany. And first when we arrived there, they wouldn't let me in. They said only the two of them can go to Sweden. And I couldn't imagine why, and the two said, then if she can't go, we don't go. So it turned out only after, in Switzerland, I gave a-- a interview, the first one they heard from someone. And as usual, a newspaper man, he wrote  completely something different. He said I'm going to Sweden, but from there, I'm going to Norway for  a political reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they read that in Swedish, they thought I was a Hungarian communist, OK? But it all cleared out. I got into Sweden, and I got to-- with his mother, my fiance's mother-- the two of us went, and she was showing everybody the newspaper, and she explained in Swedish. I couldn't speak. And we arrived to Stockholm, and there is my fiance with another man, and they're greeting me this way. And I said, what's that? He says, oh, that's a communist greeting. I thought he was joking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyways, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it didn't-- was not very good, and they left us there, and we  had to look for a place with the mother. So next day I called up the Swedish embassy, and they were so happy that I am alive and there. And I went and saw all my old friends.

words.text: and we  had to look for a

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they told me about the newspaper article, and I said, that's what's in it? I never told them such a thing. So then I said, but now I am worried. Could you please-- I know I have to marry this man, because after all, he saved me, and that's how he brought me out here and paid for everything. But he greeted me this way. Is it possible that he's a communist?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, we'll find out. They sure find out that he's one of the top communists of Sweden, and the man, he was-- he was the president of it. So they let me off the hook. I don't have to marry him. I said that's impossible that I should marry a communist now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then my father came out first, because the Protestant people helped us a lot, and papa came out. They let him out. The bishop of Hungary let him out-- helped him, and he got a lot of money to take back to build up the Protestant churches, too. That was [INAUDIBLE], because they helped us so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I kept on writing, you have to come out. You have to come out. You have to. So I got the visas, and last minute before the curtain went down, my two Russian dissidents, they also left. But they helped my parents to get out Hungary to Prague, and also my very dear girlfriend and her mother. One was already gone. So they all came to Sweden. By the time my parents came to Sweden, and my father had a wonderful job, and mother had a catering business, everybody started to learn Russian. And I said, oh, no. No. They are afraid that the Russians are going to occupy. I have to go to America. We all have to come here. But the Hungarian quarter was closed. The Australians, however, let me in. So I left my parents there. We were just there for six months or something, together, and I left for Australia because I'm going to make a lot of money in Australia. Well, I didn't know what a horribly miserable life I had for three years. I even wanted to commit suicide, what is unusual for me. But it was horrifying. But today, of course, it's different. At that time, 9:00 in the evening, you couldn't even get a cup of coffee in Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in any case, out of surprise, suddenly the Swed-- the American people called me after six years only, and you have the visa. And I says, huh? How come? He said, we found out you worked for [INAUDIBLE] during the war. So I got the visa, but I had to be in two months-- by  June, I had to be here. I had not one penny. So again, the miracles. First of all, some-- the family here, they said they will help me to pay for the cargo boat. Seven weeks from here to Boston. And I was-- I belonged to this women's organization called the Soroptimist International, and I became a soroptimist in Sydney. And that time, when a foreigner came in here as a visitor, you have to have $500 in the bank. So one of these friends said, would you take that $500 for me? And I said, oh, I'd be delighted, because I had one penny on me. So seven weeks, I spent $20 out of that. So when I arrived, finally, to New York, through Boston and all that, I had three darling friends here-- my mother's friends-- three unmarried, old gentlemen, brothers. And I told them that I have to pay $20. That's all? That's our present to you. So they put the $500 for the woman into the bank. That's how I came to America. What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what about your life since 1951?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I really came as a queen. I came here. It was incredible. I didn't owe them a thing. They welcomed me. I don't know why, but anyhow. And then my life was wonderful. First of all, I found my two girlfriends, whom I saved, and their mother was here-- everybody. And their brother, also. He finally came from England here, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- first day-- first of all, my girlfriend didn't like my red hair at that time, and it was long and all that. So I had to sit all day long in a hairdresser to fix my hair. And then I hear her talking to someone. He says, you want a mother's help? I don't know anybody, and I kicked her. I said, Alice, I need a job. Oh, yeah, yeah. Augie's here. So I went for two days to Fire island, OK? And there I was with this family, and i earned $25, what at that time, in '51, was an awful lot of money, so much so that I could pay-- at that time, foreigners needed a first paper before you even get your green card. And that was $10.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from the other one, I bought, for seven something, the Philadelphia symphony's season ticket for myself. And I went to a wedding, and I needed a little hat, because it was a religious wedding. All for $25, which was an awful lot then. I think I also paid for the magazine. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the next week, I wanted a job, and I saw that Stauffer's was looking for a hostess. And I did hostessing in Australia. My friend right away said, they don't take Jews. I said, what? In this country they're asking your religion? And she said, oh yes, I'm sure they do. So I went on 5th Avenue, and I walked in. And a very lovely lady interviewed me, and she said, have you been doing hostessing before? I said, yes, I did it in Australia, but there we also had to clean the tables and all that. She says, wonderful. You're the one we need, and you speak languages. So she sent me down. That was-- that time they an office opposite-- a restaurant opposite Grand Central Station. Now there is a bank. It's not there. And they took me immediately, and I called home, and I said, hey, kids, I got the job. No! I said, nobody asked my religion. They all asked me if I know what to do. So I was there six months, and I had a wonderful manager there, a gentleman, and he-- he wanted to get me $0.50 more a day. I earned $40 a week, but Stauffer's was so lovely. Every weekend, they give you all the food to all the helpers. And so-- and the people who came to eat-- we had-- that time we had the airline terminal right there on 42nd Street, and there was the concession couple who came always to eat there. And one day I went up, and I said, do you think you can get me a job with an airline?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were so gracious. They brought me a typewriter-- because I had three hours in the afternoon-- and the papers, and I filled them out. And I went home, and I said, Alice, I'm getting a job with the airline. The airlines don't take Jewish people, again. [INAUDIBLE] I says, let's see. Again, I went to Pan American first, and I don't know why the Pan American man said-- he said, you know, we don't pay very well. You're better off in TWA. And I went to TWA, and that was a Friday. And the man says, you can start on Monday. And I said, oh, but I have to go to Stauffer's and the manager is trying to get me $0.50.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, I went down and told my boss that [INAUDIBLE]. He said, Aggie, you got a good job? And I told him. He called up. 12 years I was with the airline. What about your romantic life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a lot of boyfriends with cars and so on, you know. But nothing serious. And do you want to know the story? My parents came in 19-- I came here, and they went to Argentina, believe it or not. And then from Argentina, Pan America brought them free. They didn't pay a penny to come here. And I got them apartment. I had a bachelor apartment here on Bird Street, and I got my parents an apartment with a bedroom and a living room. And mommy also did catering here and so forth. And I bought my father and my mother now the Philadelphia Symphony tickets. The second year, papa said, I can't come anymore. The steps are too much. You know, we were sitting all the way up top, because that was the cheapest. My dearest husband, he always wanted to come and listen to the famous Kincaid, who was a flutist-- a flutist for Philadelphia. But he didn't know that he can write and he  could get the season tickets. There were 10 concerts that time. But by chance, he stopped at the [LAUGHS] red light with a friend, and he said, you go on. I'll go in and try to buy, and he bought my father's ticket. Papa said it was written in the stars.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we always said, when somebody asking us, where did you meet, we said, Carnegie Hall. So it was a very little row-- my mother, myself, him, and an old lady, whom I knew before my parents, and we were very good friends. And the concert starts in October, and they only stopped in May. And suddenly one day, the old lady turns to my husband, and she says, young man-- because he was-- crew cut he had at that time-- she says, young man, are you Chinese? And he says, no, I'm Japanese. So I knew one thing, and I knew he likes the same music. Next month, she asks him again. She said, what are you doing in this country? He said, I am a doctor. She says, medical? My husband says, yes. OK. So February came, and I was sick, and mommy was sick. You know, my tongue was swollen.

words.text: what are you doing in this

speaker: interviewee
text: But an immigrant can't lose money, and I said, mommy, I'll go to town, and I'll sell the tickets, and I'll come right home. But I went in, and suddenly it came to, gee, the Japanese doctor will be so disappointed if I'm not coming. But it was snowing. It was cold. The place was empty, but [INAUDIBLE] was playing that night. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a man runs up to me, and he said, you have two tickets? I said, no, only one. And I sold one. I went to the phone. I said, mommy, I came all the way. I just as well stay. And I couldn't make the steps. I was really sick. He came down. He says, you sick? I said, good doctor. He can see it. [LAUGHS] So we sat down, and he asked me if want to have coffee with him after. So we sat-- it's not there anymore. There used to be an Italian coffee shop that's down in the Village now. Until 1:00 in the morning we talked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my tongue disappeared, [LAUGHS] and this fever disappeared. And we had no time for dating. We dated over the telephone. He was working at the Lebanon Hospital in the Bronx, and I worked for TWA. I had to get up 4:00 every morning, because I opened the office at east side terminal at 7:00.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we listened to Pablo Casals master classes on two telephones, two radios. [LAUGHS] Drove the family crazy. So finally, February-- we didn't have much money. We went-- that's also disappeared now. Brass Rail used to be-- finish? Go on. Yeah, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyhow, in April, we got married-- April 26, 1960. And then he turned-- when we got engaged in March, he turned to the old lady. He says, excuse me, we are getting married. And the old lady-- huh? He says, because of you, we are getting married. She started it all. And sure enough, we married, and we, courtesy through TWA, we went on a $75, three weeks honeymoon to Europe. We visited Paris first. Then we went to Sweden to meet all my old friends and everybody. After all, three years was a long time. And then we went to Switzerland to meet the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have two boys. After our second honeymoon, we went to Italy, and I had my  first one made in Italy. [LAUGHS] We brought him home. And the second one was born-- one in '63, one in '65.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in terms of your work with finding Raoul Wallenberg and the commitment that you've had to his cause as well as to him, what-- what would you like to say about that right now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is beside my family. This is my great goal, to get the truth out. Because this was the most fantastic human being you can think of. I only can talk from myself, as I said, because-- at [INAUDIBLE], because I don't know how the other helpers, who doesn't want to help us, even those who are alive. But he changed my life. He made me a caring person, a person who knows that there is no difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've been speaking. You've been organizing. You've been--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am-- all the time. All the time. And we have been-- we were very lucky. For almost seven years, we were affiliated with Queensborough Community College, and they threw us out about six months ago, what was very bad because we had no address, and I can't write a newsletter. But then a friend of mine helped, and now we are going to be affiliated with Kingsborough Community College in Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the we is the Wallenberg--

speaker: interviewee
text: Greater New York Wallenberg Committee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your work involves doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Giving awards to high school seniors who have done humanitarian work outside the school. Giving humanitarian awards to great people, like, for instance, Boys Town in Nebraska-- Father Flanagan's Boys Town. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that your life-- if it's fair for me to say this-- has been about carrying on the work of humanitarianism as you learned it through Raoul Wallenberg and want to continue in your own life in your way, to continue the helping of other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I am doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that as we're coming close to the ending of the testimony right now, thinking back on this extraordinary life that you've lived and all of the people that you've encountered-- the many, many people you've saved; surviving yourself, helping your family survive-- if you were to capture something that you would want particularly to have said about your view of survival, of the holocaust, of what you'd want the world to know from your point of view, what would you say it would be?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's very difficult to say. Just that we have to learn. We have to give. We have to care. That is the problem, that there is not enough people who are doing it. There is not enough parents who talk to their children and teaching it to them. And we want to change the world. We want peace. That's Raoul Wallenberg wanted. He wanted the children of the world to hold hands and make peace, regardless of color or religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, then you certain--

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's only through children that we can do that. That's what I saw in 1939 when we were in Marseilles-- how we could just-- we could have made peace right there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your life certainly-- not only by what you say, but how you live-- has been a representation of that, has it not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I hope. I hope. And I would like to give it as long as I live and I can be strong enough to never, never forget that this important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, on behalf of the foundation and my own personal feeling about interviewing you, I want to thank you, indeed, for this interview. And what we'll be doing now is we'll show pictures, and we'll have your family here, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I thank you very much, Mr. Spielberg, that he allows me to do that. Maybe we will hear from him. [LAUGHS] Seven year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you start over?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This [INAUDIBLE] this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was seven year old here, and I had very beautiful blue eyes and curly hair. And I had one of my little friends-- it's a little monkey, who is there photographed with me. But as you can see, it was war. Just fall down a whole floor, and you can see these little white things. That's all from the bomb what fell down and scratched it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- I was seven year old, and this little dress on me was from Austria, and when my father took us to Turkey, that was the dress I wore. And they almost sold me in it. Thank you. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my very darling mother and father, and this picture already is taken in Sweden. They had a very very lovely little apartment with a garden, and that's where we're hoping that we get together in Australia again. But it didn't work. But this is my mother and my father. Both survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is Raoul Wallenberg in Budapest, at his desk in the office when we were still up at the legation. But you can imagine he had to pick up all this machinery almost every day, and walk to another house and apartment because Eichmann was after him. But he always smiled-- always smiled.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: 32. And he didn't really look Scandinavian, because he didn't have blonde hair and blue eyes. He had very little hair, and he had brown eyes. But those brown eyes and that mouth was always smiling, except if he talked to the Nazis or the-- then he was just screaming.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you'll explain this?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Yes. So this was my father's-- father's Schutz-pass, what is a protective pass. And this, Raoul gave to people. And it said that him or her and all his belongings were protected by the Swedish government. And when we go to Sweden collectively, then after that this will be invalid.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this one, the Germans allowed him to blow up, and it was all over the walls of Budapest. And yet, the Hungarian Nazis didn't care about it, but the Germans let people go. My father was taken once, but the Germans let him go. Now, or when he pats me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just will you explain this document, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will. OK. This was our working paper, and I can't find it. We have a small one, too, but this was our working paper signed by Raoul Wallenberg. And you will have to tell that to the people that he signed nothing else, ever, but these working papers. And this, too, says in two languages that we are working for the Department B, what was Raoul's department, and the Swedish Red Cross. And as you saw, there was some Russian writing there, so evidently, when the Soviet came in, they must have stamped this. This I don't remember, but it must be so. But this one happens to be mine and all 350 of us had this paper. And it also, we didn't have to wear the star, what was wonderful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the Swedish gentleman whom I was supposed to marry, who actually saved my life. He went to the foreign department in Sweden to tell them that he would be already married with me, but the war disturbed us so they have to help me. And I became a Swedish citizen right in Hungary because of him. But by the time I arrived to Sweden, we found out that he was a top communist, and the Swedes were nice enough to let me off the hook not to have to marry a Swede-- a-- This is my first born, called Taro, what means in Japanese the first born. But he has a second name, what is John, and he is a gynecologist today. And he has a wonderful wife, and now they have a beautiful little daughter whom they adopted from Korea, as they couldn't conceive. And she's gorgeous, beautiful, and there is a great couple. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Bonnie. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is our second born, called Jiro, what means the second. [LAUGHTER] And his second name is Paul.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're not on yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm not on yet? But he's somebody. [INAUDIBLE] OK. Shall I keep going? You had the beginning? All right. So he married Amy. Jiro is a writer, and he's also teaching English as a second language for grown ups, what he enjoys tremendously. And Amy is an artist. She's making beautiful stuff, and she has a lot of exhibitions, and they don't have a baby yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were they married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll explain, then, this award, one of many that that you have here.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was one of many, but this was a great surprise, and it really came out unexpected. And it was a very wonderful affair. It was in Yorkville, what is a Hungarian churches-- the Catholic church, a Protestant church. And this church where we had these, it's very funny because it's run by a wonderful priest who is from Ireland. [LAUGHS] Doesn't speak Hungarian. But they made a candlelight thing for me. We walked with a candle, and there were about 10 different priests. There was even a black gentleman there. And they really gave me this beautiful award, and I was very, very proud, and very, very excited, too. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was for the thousands of Jewish lives that you had saved. This was the award.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and what I am still doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what you're still doing. OK. Would you like to introduce your husband? And perhaps, if you have anything you'd like to say, this would be the time do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my husband, Dr. Masazumi Adachi. But really, you should say what it means in Japanese and how you lost your purity, because that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a funny story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's a very interesting story. When he arrived here first, he's a doctor-- a medical doctor. He is a neuropathologist. And also a great researcher in cancer and in brain. And hopefully one day he will get the Nobel Prize, because he's wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you say something about your wife and about her story of survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's a very fascinating story. I wonder whether she told her story. We went honeymoon in Stockholm. We were at the hotel. She's telling the whole story. [INAUDIBLE] this story wasn't fiction. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he cried right through it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Amazing story. Yeah. So after that, she was working hard, and things came out. So now, she's helping especially children to put motivation. She was saying that one person can do so much. That's a great mission.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
